learn.title: Coding-Decoding
learn.intro: Every coding-decoding question hides one simple, mechanical rule behind a worked example. The entire skill is decoding that rule correctly using every letter of the example, then applying it exactly ŌĆö no creativity needed, just careful arithmetic.

sections.explanation: Each letter is replaced by another letter or number based on a fixed rule ŌĆö usually its position in the alphabet, or a fixed offset from it. The key skill is decoding the rule from an example, then applying it to a new word.
sections.realLifeExample: A simple substitution cipher, where every letter is systematically replaced, works exactly like this ŌĆö old military or spy codes used offset alphabets for basic message secrecy.
sections.memoryTrick: Letters are just numbers in disguise: A=1, B=2, ... Z=26 ŌĆö convert to numbers first, do the arithmetic, then convert back.
sections.shortcutTrick: Write out the alphabet with numbers underneath (A1 B2 C3...) once at the start of the question, and refer back to it instead of counting on your fingers each time.
formulaCard: Coded letter = original position ┬▒ fixed offset (wrapping from 26 back to 1 if needed)
commonMistakes: Losing count of a letter's position, especially for letters past M.
Forgetting to wrap around (after Z, the alphabet cycles back to A) when adding an offset near the end.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's alphabet coding usually gives one worked example ŌĆö figure out the letter-by-letter shift from it first, then apply the same shift to the new word.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT sometimes codes using the alphabet's REVERSE position (A=26, Z=1) instead of the forward one ŌĆö always verify direction using the given example before assuming forward coding.

sections.explanation: Letter shift coding moves each letter forward or backward by a fixed number of places in the alphabet (a Caesar shift). The shift amount is usually constant for the whole word.
sections.realLifeExample: This is exactly the classic 'Caesar cipher' used in ancient Rome to send military messages ŌĆö shift every letter by a fixed number of places.
sections.memoryTrick: Shift right = move forward in the alphabet (add); shift left = move backward (subtract).
sections.shortcutTrick: Once you know the shift amount from an example, apply it directly to every letter of the new word without re-deriving it ŌĆö the shift stays constant within a single question unless stated otherwise.
formulaCard: Forward shift by n: new position = (original position + n ŌłÆ 1) mod 26 + 1
Backward shift by n: new position = (original position ŌłÆ n ŌłÆ 1 + 26) mod 26 + 1
commonMistakes: Assuming the shift changes letter to letter without checking whether the pattern actually is meant to change.
Miscounting the wrap-around at the alphabet boundary.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint sometimes uses an increasing shift (each letter shifts by 1 more than the previous) ŌĆö check at least 2-3 letters of the example before assuming a constant shift.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT's letter-shift questions are usually the fastest points in the section if the shift is constant ŌĆö verify with just the first 2 letters, then apply mechanically to the rest.

sections.explanation: Reverse coding writes the letters of a word in reverse order, sometimes combined with a shift or position-based rule on top of the reversal.
sections.realLifeExample: Writing your name backward as a childhood 'secret code' is exactly reverse coding ŌĆö most words become a completely different-looking string when reversed.
sections.memoryTrick: Reverse coding is a mirror ŌĆö read the original word's letters from right to left.
sections.shortcutTrick: When a question combines reversal with a shift, do the reversal first, then apply the shift to the already-reversed letters ŌĆö but always check the given example to confirm this order, since some questions do it the other way around.
formulaCard: Reverse of a word: read all letters right to left
Combined reverse+shift: confirm order of operations using the example given
commonMistakes: Applying a shift before reversing when the example shows reversal happening first, or vice versa.
Miscounting letters when reversing longer words, especially with repeated letters.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's reverse coding questions often hide the reversal inside a longer combined rule ŌĆö write the reversed word out fully before applying any further rule.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT sometimes reverses only part of a word (like reversing pairs of letters) ŌĆö read the given example extremely carefully before assuming a full-word reversal.

sections.explanation: Position-based coding replaces or rearranges letters based on their exact position within the word (1st, 2nd, 3rd...) rather than their position in the alphabet.
sections.realLifeExample: A locker code that swaps the 1st and 3rd digits of your birth year is a simple form of position-based encoding ŌĆö order within the sequence matters more than the digit's own value.
sections.memoryTrick: Position-based coding cares about WHERE a letter sits in the word, not WHAT letter it is.
sections.shortcutTrick: Number each letter of the word by its position (1st, 2nd, 3rd...) before applying any rule ŌĆö this prevents confusing 'position in the word' with 'position in the alphabet.'
formulaCard: Position in word ŌēĀ position in alphabet ŌĆö always confirm which one a rule refers to
Common rules: swap adjacent or symmetric positions, or apply a different shift to each position
commonMistakes: Confusing a letter's position within the WORD with its position within the ALPHABET.
Miscounting positions in words with repeated letters.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's position-based questions often swap specific positions (like 1st and last) ŌĆö write numbered positions above each letter before making any swaps.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT sometimes applies a DIFFERENT shift to each position (1st letter +1, 2nd letter +2...) ŌĆö verify this pattern using at least the first 3 letters of the given example.

sections.explanation: Mixed coding combines two or more of the rules above (shift, reversal, position-based rules) into a single question, requiring you to identify and apply each rule in the correct sequence.
sections.realLifeExample: Modern multi-layer encryption combines several simple transformations in sequence, exactly like a mixed-coding question ŌĆö no single step is complex, but the combination needs careful, ordered execution.
sections.memoryTrick: Mixed coding is just multiple simple codes stacked ŌĆö solve them one layer at a time, never all at once.
sections.shortcutTrick: Write down each transformation as a separate, labeled step (Step 1: shift, Step 2: reverse...) based on what the example shows, then apply the same sequence to the new word.
formulaCard: Identify each individual rule separately from the given example first
Apply the rules in the same order shown by the example ŌĆö order changes the result
commonMistakes: Trying to apply all the rules simultaneously in your head instead of one clear step at a time.
Applying the rules in a different order than the example demonstrates.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's mixed coding questions are usually worth extra time ŌĆö budget 45-60 seconds to carefully separate out each individual rule before applying anything.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT often re-uses the exact same combined rule across 2-3 questions ŌĆö once decoded correctly, apply it directly to the rest without re-deriving it.

sections.explanation: Some coding questions don't state an explicit rule ŌĆö instead, you must spot the numeric or alphabetic pattern directly from a sequence of coded examples, similar to a mini number-series question but with letters.
sections.realLifeExample: Recognizing that house numbers on a street increase by a fixed pattern (odd on one side, even on the other) is the same skill as spotting a coding pattern from limited examples.
sections.memoryTrick: No stated rule means you're the codebreaker ŌĆö look for what changes consistently between each given example.
sections.shortcutTrick: Convert every letter to its alphabet-position number first ŌĆö patterns that look confusing in letters often become obvious once written as numbers.
formulaCard: Convert letters to numbers first ŌĆö patterns are almost always easier to spot numerically
Check position-wise differences between corresponding letters across multiple examples
commonMistakes: Trying to spot the pattern directly in letter form instead of converting to numbers first.
Assuming a pattern from just one example instead of confirming it across all given examples.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint's pattern-recognition questions often give 2-3 worked examples specifically so you can cross-verify your guessed rule ŌĆö always check against every example, not just the first.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT sometimes mixes a numeric pattern with an alphabetic one in the same sequence ŌĆö separate the numbers and letters into two parallel patterns rather than solving them as one.

solvedExamples.question: If CAT is coded as DBU, how is DOG coded using the same rule?
solvedExamples.solution:
C(3)ŌåÆD(4), A(1)ŌåÆB(2), T(20)ŌåÆU(21) ŌĆö each letter shifts forward by 1.
Applying the same rule: D(4)ŌåÆE(5), O(15)ŌåÆP(16), G(7)ŌåÆH(8).

solvedExamples.question: In a certain code, each letter is shifted back by 3 places. How is HELLO coded?
If the code for WORLD is DLROW, how is PLANET coded using the same rule?
solvedExamples.solution:
The rule simply reverses the word: WORLDŌåÆDLROW confirms this.
Reversing PLANET letter by letter gives TENALP.

solvedExamples.question: In a code, the 1st and last letters of a word are swapped, and the middle letters stay unchanged. How is TIGER coded?
solvedExamples.solution: TIGER: 1st=T, last=R. Swapping these two: RIGET.

solvedExamples.question: If MANGO is coded as OCPIQ by shifting each letter forward by 2 places, how is APPLE coded using the same rule?
solvedExamples.solution:
Confirm the rule: M(13)ŌåÆO(15), A(1)ŌåÆC(3), N(14)ŌåÆP(16), G(7)ŌåÆI(9), O(15)ŌåÆQ(17) ŌĆö each letter +2.
Applying to APPLE: AŌåÆC, PŌåÆR, PŌåÆR, LŌåÆN, EŌåÆG.

practiceExamples.question: If BOOK is coded as CPPL, how is PAGE coded using the same rule?
practiceExamples.hint: Find the shift from BOOKŌåÆCPPL (each letter +1), then apply it to PAGE.

practiceExamples.question: Each letter in a word is shifted forward by 4 places. How is CODE coded?
If MUSIC is coded as CISUM, how is TABLE coded?
practiceExamples.hint: Simply reverse the letters of TABLE.

practiceExamples.question: In a code, the 2nd and 4th letters of a 5-letter word are swapped. How is CHAIR coded?
practiceExamples.hint: CHAIR positions: 1-C, 2-H, 3-A, 4-I, 5-R; swap positions 2 and 4 (H and I).

practiceExamples.question: If FOX is coded as XOF (reversed) and then each letter is shifted back by 1, how is CAT coded using the same rule?
practiceExamples.hint: First reverse CAT to get TAC, then shift each letter back by 1 (remember A wraps to Z).

easy.question: If APPLE is coded as BQQMF (each letter shifted forward by 1), how is MANGO coded using the same rule?
easy.explanation: The rule shifts each letter forward by 1 (confirmed across all of APPLEŌåÆBQQMF). Applying +1 to MANGO: MŌåÆN, AŌåÆB, NŌåÆO, GŌåÆH, OŌåÆP, giving NBOHP.
whyWrong: OCPIQ results from a +2 shift, not the +1 shift shown in the example.
LZMFN results from a ŌłÆ1 shift, the wrong direction.
NBOPH has two letters out of order compared to the correct shift result.
easy.shortcut: Verify the shift direction and amount using every letter of the given example, not just the first one.

easy.question: If each letter in a word is shifted back by 2 places, how is FLOWER coded?
easy.explanation: Shifting each letter of FLOWER back by 2: FŌåÆD, LŌåÆJ, OŌåÆM, WŌåÆU, EŌåÆC, RŌåÆP, giving DJMUCP.
whyWrong: HNQYGT results from shifting forward by 2, the wrong direction.
DJMUCQ has the last letter wrong ŌĆö R shifted back by 2 gives P, not Q.
EJMUCP has the first letter wrong ŌĆö F shifted back by 2 gives D, not E.
easy.shortcut: Shift 'back' always means subtract from the letter's position, wrapping from A back to Z if needed.

easy.question: If the code for CHAIR is RIAHC, how is TABLE coded using the same rule?
easy.explanation: The rule simply reverses the letters: CHAIRŌåÆRIAHC confirms this. Reversing TABLE gives ELBAT.
whyWrong: ETBLA doesn't match the letters of TABLE in reverse order.
TABEL is not a reversal at all ŌĆö it's the original word with two letters swapped.
ELBTA has the last two letters swapped compared to a true reversal.
easy.shortcut: Write the word backward letter by letter, double-checking against the given example's pattern.

easy.question: In a code, the first and last letters of a word are swapped, and the middle letters stay unchanged. How is WATER coded?
easy.explanation: Swapping only the first (W) and last (R) letters of WATER, keeping ATE unchanged in the middle, gives RATEW.
whyWrong: RETAW rearranges more letters than just the first and last.
WATER is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap was applied.
RAWET incorrectly rearranges the middle letters as well.
easy.shortcut: Identify only the two positions the rule affects, and leave every other letter exactly where it is.

easy.question: If B is coded as 2, D is coded as 4, and F is coded as 6, what is H coded as?
easy.explanation: Each letter is coded as its own position in the alphabet (B=2nd, D=4th, F=6th). H is the 8th letter, so it's coded as 8.
whyWrong: 7 doesn't match H's actual alphabet position.
9 doesn't match it either.
6 was F's code, not H's.
easy.shortcut: Convert each letter to its alphabet position number ŌĆö the pattern becomes obvious immediately.

medium.question: If TIGER is coded as WLJHU, how is LION coded using the same rule?
medium.explanation: TIGERŌåÆWLJHU shows each letter shifted forward by 3. Applying the same +3 shift to LION: LŌåÆO, IŌåÆL, OŌåÆR, NŌåÆQ, giving OLRQ.
whyWrong: MJPO results from a +1 shift, not the +3 shift shown in the example.
OLRP has the last letter wrong ŌĆö N shifted by 3 gives Q, not P.
NKQP results from a +2 shift, still not matching the example.
medium.shortcut: Check the shift amount using at least 2 letters of the example to rule out coincidental matches.

medium.question: If each letter in a word is shifted back by 4 places, how is MONKEY coded?
medium.explanation: Shifting each letter of MONKEY back by 4: MŌåÆI, OŌåÆK, NŌåÆJ, KŌåÆG, EŌåÆA, YŌåÆU, giving IKJGAU.
whyWrong: QSRODC results from shifting forward by 4, the wrong direction.
IKJGAV has the last letter wrong ŌĆö Y shifted back by 4 gives U, not V.
HKJGAU has the first letter wrong ŌĆö M shifted back by 4 gives I, not H.
medium.shortcut: For a backward shift near the start of the alphabet, double-check whether wrap-around is actually needed.

medium.question: If the code for GARDEN is NEDRAG, how is KITCHEN coded using the same rule?
medium.explanation: GARDENŌåÆNEDRAG confirms a simple reversal rule. Reversing KITCHEN letter by letter gives NEHCTIK.
whyWrong: NEHCTKI has the last two letters swapped compared to a true reversal.
KEHCTIN keeps the first letter of the original word instead of moving it to the end.
NEHTCIK has two middle letters swapped out of the correct reversed order.
medium.shortcut: For longer words, write the letters in a column and read upward to reverse accurately without missing any.

medium.question: In a code, the first and last letters of a word are swapped. How is BRIDGE coded?
medium.explanation: Swapping only the first (B) and last (E) letters of BRIDGE, keeping RIDG unchanged in the middle, gives ERIDGB.
whyWrong: EBIRDG rearranges more letters than just the first and last.
GRIDEB swaps the wrong pair of letters.
ERIDBG has the middle letters in the wrong order.
medium.shortcut: Identify only the two positions the rule affects, and leave every other letter exactly where it is.

medium.question: In a code, the 2nd and 5th letters of a word are swapped. How is CANDLE coded?
medium.explanation: CANDLE has letters C-A-N-D-L-E at positions 1-6. Swapping the 2nd (A) and 5th (L) letters gives C-L-N-D-A-E, i.e. CLNDAE.
whyWrong: CANDLE is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
CADNLE swaps the wrong pair of adjacent letters instead of positions 2 and 5.
CLNDLE incorrectly duplicates a letter instead of swapping properly.
medium.shortcut: Number each letter's position above the word before making any swap, to avoid counting errors in longer words.

medium.question: If WINDOW is coded as YQFPKY by first shifting each letter forward by 2 and then reversing the result, how is SIMPLE coded using the same rule?
medium.explanation: Shift SIMPLE forward by 2 first: UKORNG. Then reverse that result: GNROKU.
whyWrong: UOKRNG is the shifted word without being reversed.
GNROKV has the last letter wrong.
GNOKRU has two middle letters swapped compared to the correct reversal.
medium.shortcut: In a mixed rule, always perform the steps in the exact order the example demonstrates ŌĆö shift first, then reverse, here.

medium.question: If SIMPLE is coded as GNROKU by first reversing the word and then shifting each letter forward by 2, how is GARDEN coded using the same rule?
medium.explanation: Reverse GARDEN first: NEDRAG. Then shift each letter forward by 2: PGFTCI.
whyWrong: ICTFGP is the reversed word without the shift applied.
PGFTCJ has the last letter wrong.
QHGUDJ results from shifting by 3 instead of 2.
medium.shortcut: Notice this example reverses FIRST and shifts SECOND ŌĆö the opposite order from the previous question ŌĆö always re-check the order from scratch for each new example.

medium.question: In a code, the 2nd and 5th letters of a word are swapped. How is MARKET coded?
medium.explanation: MARKET has letters M-A-R-K-E-T at positions 1-6. Swapping the 2nd (A) and 5th (E) letters gives M-E-R-K-A-T, i.e. MERKAT.
whyWrong: MARKET is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
METKAR rearranges more letters than the specified swap.
MERKTA has the last two letters also swapped, which wasn't part of the rule.
medium.shortcut: Only swap the two specified positions ŌĆö every other letter, including ones that look similar, stays exactly where it was.

medium.question: In a code, the 1st letter of a word shifts forward by 1, the 2nd by 2, the 3rd by 3, and so on. How is PLANE coded?
medium.explanation: P(+1)=Q, L(+2)=N, A(+3)=D, N(+4)=R, E(+5)=J, giving QNDRJ.
whyWrong: QNEUJ has the 3rd and 4th letters shifted by the wrong amounts.
QMDRJ has the 2nd letter shifted by only 1 instead of 2.
QNDSJ has the 4th letter shifted by 5 instead of 4.
medium.shortcut: Write the shift amount (1,2,3,4,5...) above each letter's position before doing any arithmetic, so you never lose track of which shift applies where.

medium.question: If B is coded as 4, C is coded as 9, and D is coded as 16, what is E coded as?
medium.explanation: Each letter's alphabet position is squared: B(2)ŌåÆ4, C(3)ŌåÆ9, D(4)ŌåÆ16. E is the 5th letter, so its code is 5┬▓=25.
whyWrong: 20 doesn't match 5┬▓ at all.
24 is one less than the correct square.
22 doesn't follow the squaring pattern either.
medium.shortcut: Convert each letter to its alphabet position first, then look for the numeric relationship ŌĆö squaring becomes obvious immediately in number form.

hard.question: If COMPUTER is coded as HTRUZYJW using a fixed forward shift, how is KEYBOARD coded using the same rule?
hard.explanation: COMPUTERŌåÆHTRUZYJW confirms a forward shift of 5 (CŌåÆH is +5). Applying +5 to KEYBOARD: KŌåÆP, EŌåÆJ, YŌåÆD, BŌåÆG, OŌåÆT, AŌåÆF, RŌåÆW, DŌåÆI, giving PJDGTFWI.
whyWrong: PJDGTFVI has the last letter wrong ŌĆö D shifted by 5 gives I, not V.
OICFSEVH results from a +4 shift, not the +5 shift shown in the example.
PJEGTFWI has the 3rd letter wrong ŌĆö Y shifted by 5 gives D, not E.
hard.shortcut: Verify the shift using at least the first 3 letters of a long example before committing to a shift amount.

hard.question: If the code for ELEPHANT is TNAHPELE, how is DAUGHTER coded using the same rule?
hard.explanation: ELEPHANTŌåÆTNAHPELE confirms a simple full-word reversal. Reversing DAUGHTER gives RETHGUAD.
whyWrong: RETHGAUD has two middle letters swapped compared to the true reversal.
DAUGHTER is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no reversal was applied.
RETHGUDA has the last two letters swapped compared to the correct reversal.
hard.shortcut: For 8-letter words, write both halves separately and reverse each before combining, to avoid losing track midway.

hard.question: If TRIANGLE is coded as ERIANGLT by swapping only its first and last letters, how is JOURNEY coded using the same rule?
hard.explanation: Swapping only the first (J) and last (Y) letters of JOURNEY, keeping OURNE unchanged in the middle, gives YOURNEJ.
whyWrong: JOURNEY is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
YOURNJE has the middle letters rearranged as well, beyond just the first/last swap.
EOURNJY doesn't correctly place either the original first or last letter.
hard.shortcut: Even for longer words, this rule only ever touches 2 positions ŌĆö resist the urge to rearrange anything else.

hard.question: If SQUARE is coded as BOXRNP by first reversing the word and then shifting each letter back by 3, how is CIRCLE coded using the same rule?
hard.explanation: Reverse CIRCLE first: ELCRIC. Then shift each letter back by 3: BIZOFZ.
whyWrong: ELCRIC is the reversed word without the shift applied.
BIZOFY has the last letter wrong.
BHZOFZ has the first letter wrong ŌĆö E shifted back by 3 gives B, not H.
hard.shortcut: Reverse first, then shift ŌĆö exactly the order confirmed by the SQUAREŌåÆBOXRNP example.

hard.question: If CIRCLE is coded as HOFULF by first shifting each letter forward by 3 and then reversing the result, how is SQUARE coded using the same rule?
hard.explanation: Shift SQUARE forward by 3 first: VTXDUH. Then reverse that result: HUDXTV.
whyWrong: VTXDUH is the shifted word without being reversed.
HUDXTW has the last letter wrong.
GUDXTV has the first letter wrong.
hard.shortcut: Shift first, then reverse ŌĆö the opposite order from the previous question, confirmed by the CIRCLEŌåÆHOFULF example. Always re-verify order per question.

hard.question: If ACE is coded as BEH using an increasing shift (1st letter +1, 2nd letter +2, 3rd letter +3), how is CEG coded using the same rule?
whyWrong: DGI has the last letter wrong ŌĆö G shifted by 3 gives J, not I.
DFJ has the 2nd letter wrong ŌĆö E shifted by 2 gives G, not F.
EGJ has the 1st letter wrong ŌĆö C shifted by 1 gives D, not E.
hard.shortcut: Label each letter's position number above it (1st, 2nd, 3rd) before applying the matching shift amount.

hard.question: In a code, the first and last letters of a word are swapped. How is MOUNTAIN coded?
hard.explanation: Swapping only the first (M) and last (N) letters of MOUNTAIN, keeping OUNTAI unchanged in the middle, gives NOUNTAIM.
whyWrong: MOUNTAIN is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
NIATNUOM is a full reversal of the word, not just a first/last swap.
NOUNTAMI has the last two letters of the middle section swapped as well, beyond the specified rule.
hard.shortcut: Double-check that only the two specified positions change ŌĆö everything else, including letters that look easy to shift, must stay put.

hard.question: In a 7-letter code, the 3rd and 5th letters of a word are swapped. How is STATION coded?
hard.explanation: STATION has letters S-T-A-T-I-O-N at positions 1-7. Swapping the 3rd (A) and 5th (I) letters gives S-T-I-T-A-O-N, i.e. STITAON.
whyWrong: STATION is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
STAITON swaps the wrong pair of adjacent letters instead of positions 3 and 5.
SIATTON incorrectly moves the letter from position 1 as well.
hard.shortcut: Number every position in longer words explicitly ŌĆö with 7 letters, it's easy to miscount by one without writing the positions down.

hard.question: In a code, the first and last letters of a word are swapped. How is COMPUTER coded?
hard.explanation: Swapping only the first (C) and last (R) letters of COMPUTER, keeping OMPUTE unchanged in the middle, gives ROMPUTEC.
whyWrong: COMPUTER is the unchanged original word ŌĆö no swap applied.
RETUPMOC is a full reversal, not just a first/last swap.
ROMPUTCE has the last two middle letters swapped as well, beyond the specified rule.
hard.shortcut: For long words especially, resist reversing the whole thing when the rule only specifies a single first/last swap.

hard.question: If B is coded as 8 and C is coded as 27, what is D coded as?
hard.explanation: Each letter's alphabet position is cubed: B(2)ŌåÆ2┬│=8, C(3)ŌåÆ3┬│=27. D is the 4th letter, so its code is 4┬│=64.
whyWrong: 36 doesn't match 4┬│ at all.
48 doesn't follow the cubing pattern either.
81 is 3Ōü┤, not 4┬│ ŌĆö a different exponent pattern than the one shown.
hard.shortcut: Once two examples both match a cube pattern (2┬│=8, 3┬│=27), test the next letter's cube directly rather than guessing a different rule.

quickSheet: Convert letters to alphabet-position numbers (A=1...Z=26) before doing any arithmetic ŌĆö patterns and shifts are far easier to spot in numbers.
Verify a shift or rule using EVERY letter of the given example, not just the first one.
For mixed rules, identify each individual step separately, then apply them in the exact order the example shows.
Position-based rules only touch the specified positions ŌĆö never rearrange anything else.
When no rule is stated, convert to numbers and look for a consistent numeric relationship (add, multiply, square, cube) across all given examples.
oneMinute: Shift, reverse, and position-swap are the three basic moves ŌĆö everything else is a combination of these.
Wrap-around matters: after Z, the alphabet cycles back to A.
Re-verify the rule's order of operations for every new mixed-coding example ŌĆö it can flip between questions.
revision.summary: Coding-decoding rewards mechanical discipline over cleverness. Convert letters to numbers, extract the rule using every letter of the given example (not just a convenient one), and for mixed or pattern-based questions, work through each layer as a separate, labeled step. Speed comes from not re-deriving a rule you've already confirmed, and accuracy comes from never skipping the verification step.
